nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, intertidal rocky shores, and hard-bottom areas with abundant empty gastropod shells. Typically associated with coral rubble, turf algae, and sand patches in calm lagoonal water.

decorPreferences: established live rock with crevices for grazing and hiding
ample empty gastropod shells of varied sizes (provided spare)
mature reef with surface algae films

diet.notes: Has been documented as a voracious scavenger consuming detritus, hair algae, film algae, cyanobacteria, leftover food, and decaying organic matter. Has also been reported to graze on softer fleshy macroalgae (Ulva, Halymenia, scroll algae). If the tank is too clean, generally advised to supplement with sinking pellets, dried seaweed/nori, or small bits of meaty seafood once or twice a week.

goodWith: most peaceful reef fish (gobies, blennies, cardinalfish, clownfish, small wrasses)
conspecifics and other small reef-safe hermit species
established reef tanks with corals (LPS, SPS, soft)
avoidWith: predatory fish that target small crustaceans (large wrasses, hawkfish, triggers, puffers, large hogfish)
aggressive crabs (Xanthid, Calcinus laevimanus, larger hermits)
small or thin-shelled snails when spare shells are not provided
expensive ornamental snails kept without backup shells

summary: Tiny Caribbean hermit (~1–2.5 cm) with blue legs banded in red — the workhorse saltwater cleanup-crew hermit. Has been documented eating hair algae, cyanobacteria, and detritus. Generally peaceful but will attack snails for shells if spares aren't provided.
careNotes: Generally considered the entry-level marine cleanup-crew hermit and is among the most commonly stocked invertebrates in reef aquaria. Small adult size (~1–2.5 cm) lets it work crevices in live rock that larger hermits and snails can't reach, while still consuming nuisance hair algae, film algae, cyanobacteria, detritus, and uneaten food. Has been reported to be relatively peaceful toward tankmates but will fight conspecifics and attack snails to acquire larger shells as it grows — common practice is to keep a small group (3+) and provide a varied assortment of empty gastropod shells (especially Astraea-, Trochus-, and Nerite-sized) so each crab has upgrade options. Without spare shells, snail losses and hermit-on-hermit shell evictions tend to increase. Like all marine crustaceans this species is highly intolerant of copper-based medications and is sensitive to abrupt salinity, temperature, and pH swings — drip-acclimate carefully on arrival and never dose copper into the display. Generally considered reef-safe; isolated reports exist of nipping at SPS polyps when underfed, but this is not the typical behavior. Cross-source notes: temperature 22–26°C (LiveAquaria 72–78°F, Aquariumbreeder 22–26°C); pH 8.0–8.5 across sources, averaged to 8.1–8.4; salinity 1.018–1.025 SG (Aquariumbreeder) and 1.020–1.025 SG (LiveAquaria); dKH 8–12 (LiveAquaria) vs 8–16 (Aquariumbreeder), kept at 8–12 as the reef-typical range; adult size widely stated at ~1 inch / 2 cm (LiveAquaria, Wikipedia, Reefcleaners.org) with Aquariumbreeder citing up to 2.5 cm — used 1–2.5 cm. Minimum tank: LiveAquaria recommends ~10 gal (≈38 L), Aquariumbreeder cites ~20 L — used 40 L as a nice nano-reef floor. Reef2Reef Wiki (designated secondary) returned 403; substituted Aquariumbreeder.com and Wikipedia as confirming secondaries; documented here for reviewer.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to be very difficult to breed in captivity, with no widely documented hobbyist successes. Like most marine hermits, the species has a planktonic larval stage requiring rearing tanks with specific microalgae and rotifer feeds, and larval survival in home setups is generally poor. Sexual dimorphism is minor; males are reported to be slightly larger than females.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirms family Diogenidae, max size ~1 inch, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, care level easy, peaceful temperament, reef-safe, omnivorous scavenger feeding on hair algae and cyanobacteria, recommended minimum 10 gal (≈38 L) with live rock and sand substrate, may attack snails for shells if undersupplied.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 on root and had no dedicated species wiki page on search. Swapped to Aquariumbreeder.com, Wikipedia, and Reefcleaners.org as confirming secondaries; URL retained for reviewer.

additional.name: Aquariumbreeder.com
additional.notes: Secondary substitute. Confirms Caribbean / W. Atlantic origin, shallow intertidal habitat (≤2 m), up to 2.5 cm adult size, lifespan up to 2 years, temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.018–1.025, dKH 8–16, omnivorous scavenger, almost completely reef-safe with rare SPS-nipping reports, semi-aggressive in shell competition, very difficult to breed in captivity.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Clibanarius tricolor
additional.notes: Taxonomic and natural-history confirmation. Family Diogenidae, order Decapoda, Caribbean shallow water (~30 cm), reaches ~2 cm, nocturnal-leaning feeding behavior, well-documented shell-fighting behavior (flipping/eviction of conspecifics).

additional.name: Reef Cleaners — Blue Leg Hermit Crab
additional.notes: Secondary. Confirms max ~25 mm, Caribbean origin, diet of detritus / hair algae / film algae / some cyanobacteria plus softer fleshy algae (scroll, halymenia, ulva), reef-safe, social/peaceful in groups, will attack snails and other hermits for shells — recommends supplying extra empty shells.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Generally molts every few weeks while growing; discarded exoskeletons are commonly consumed for calcium reabsorption. Stable salinity, calcium, and trace iodine are widely cited as important for successful molts. Hermits are particularly vulnerable during and immediately after molting; ample hiding spots in live rock are advised.
crustacean.breedingNotes: See top-level breedingNotes — planktonic larvae and specialized rearing requirements have effectively prevented hobbyist captive breeding.
